text:
Centre for Epilepsy

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 25 May 2016 

Dr James Rowe  
Centre for Epilepsy  
Queen Square, London WC1N 3BG  

Dear Dr James Rowe,                                                                                                         

Daniel Harper, DOB: 22-11-1992, Hospital No: H673920 NHS No. 9842157630  
Flat 3, 47 Merton Road, London SW18 5JY  

Focal Epilepsy 

Occupation and context: Works as a line cook in a commercial kitchen, with prolonged shifts at high heat, frequent exposure to flashing heat lamps, and rapid task transitions. Events occur in the kitchen during service. 

Medication: Patient-reported current regimen includes carbamazepine 200 mg twice daily started February 2016; using buccal midazolam 10 mg as rescue per workplace protocol (kept in manager’s office, not used to date). No known adverse drug reactions reported today aside from occasional mild dizziness at the end of long evening shifts. 

Carbamazepine Blood Level:  
18 May 2016: 6.8 mg/L (laboratory reference 4–12 mg/L). Dose 200 mg twice daily at time of sample. 

Seizures and semiology: The patient has focal epilepsy. Typical focal impaired-awareness events begin with a rising epigastric sensation and metallic taste, followed by behavioural arrest, right-hand automatisms, and brief non-responsiveness lasting 45–60 seconds, with post-event fatigue of approximately 15 minutes. Events have been witnessed by colleagues under heat lamps on the pass during peak service. His mother notes an overall improvement compared with previous years, with eight tonic seizures documented in 2016 so far. These tonic events have been brief (under 30 seconds) and occurred after clusters of focal episodes on exceptionally hot, high-stress evenings; no generalised tonic–clonic seizures reported this year. No reported auras preceding the tonic events; focal auras persist prior to impaired-awareness episodes. 

Workplace factors: Dehydration risk acknowledged; he now carries electrolyte drinks and rotates away from the grill when prodromal symptoms occur, per manager support. Staff are aware of the rescue plan and recovery position protocol. No injuries sustained; one near-miss with a hot pan was avoided by colleague intervention. 

Investigations: Prior MRI (2014) reportedly normal; routine EEG (2015) showed left temporal intermittent rhythmic slowing without definite epileptiform discharges (patient report; formal report not to hand today). 

Plan discussed today by telephone review with patient and his mother present:  
- Continue carbamazepine 200 mg twice daily; adherence emphasised with dosing after pre-shift meal and at bedtime to minimise dizziness during service.  
- Maintain hydration and scheduled brief cool-down breaks as agreed with employer.  
- Rescue buccal midazolam to remain accessible; no use to date.  
- Request copy of prior MRI/EEG and consider repeat EEG with sleep deprivation to better characterise focal onset if events persist.  
- Safety: continue heat-exposure precautions, avoid carrying heavy pans when aura occurs, and ensure a colleague escorts him off the line during episodes.  
- Review in 3 months or earlier if frequency increases or injuries occur. 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: eight tonic seizures documented in 2016 so far
His mother notes an overall improvement compared with previous years, with eight tonic seizures documented in 2016 so far

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states: "eight tonic seizures documented in 2016 so far." The clinic date is 25 May 2016, so the observation window is from 1 Jan 2016 to 25 May 2016, approximately 5 months. No other seizure type has a quantifiable frequency. Therefore, the current measurable frequency is 8 seizures over ~5 months, yielding 8 per 5 month.
seizure_frequency_number: eight tonic seizures documented in 2016 so far
reference: His mother notes an overall improvement compared with previous years, with eight tonic seizures documented in 2016 so far
